topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp white paper lay on the kitchen counter, a stark contrast to the warm, familiar scent of brewing coffee. Elias traced the lines of the genetic report with a trembling finger. Each percentage, each matching marker, felt like a tiny, sharp shard of glass digging into his chest. He reread the section detailing the biological match, his breath catching in shallow gasps. The silence of the apartment magnified the frantic drumming in his ears. He pushed the chair back so forcefully it scraped across the hardwood floor, the sound echoing loudly in the sudden stillness. His hands began to shake, making the report flutter precariously. He needed air, desperately, but the room felt too small, too confining.

[story 2]

Sarah sat on the edge of her bed, the phone screen illuminating the raw data in front of her. The colors on the screen swam, blurring the edges of the text. She kept rubbing her temples, a dull ache spreading behind her eyes. Every time she glanced at the results, a wave of nausea rolled through her, making her grip the phone tighter until her knuckles were white. She stood up abruptly, pacing the length of her living room three times before collapsing onto the sofa. A choked sound escaped her throat, a small, wounded noise. She stared blankly at the ceiling, unable to process the sudden, seismic shift in her understanding of her own history.

[story 3]

Mark stared at the DNA readout, his jaw tight enough to crack. He kept blinking, trying to force his vision to focus on the neat columns of information, but the numbers swam into an incomprehensible mess. He stood by the window, pulling the curtains aside just enough to let a sliver of harsh afternoon light hit his face. He let out a long, ragged exhale, the sound thin and reedy. He turned away from the window, his shoulders slumping forward as if the weight of the revelation had physically settled onto him. He walked slowly toward the bookshelf, his movements heavy and uncoordinated, before stopping dead, unable to bring himself to look at anything else.
